results.judged_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in two applications (application numbers one and two) against the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by two nationals of the respondent State, the First Applicant (introduced on 4 May 1998) and the Second Applicant (introduced on 12 August 1998).

The United Kingdom Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, a representative of the Government of the Foreign and Commonwealth Office.

The applicants alleged that the proceedings in which they were sued in respect of property in Florida were not dealt with within a reasonable time.

The applications were transmitted to the Court on 1 November 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The applications were allocated to the Third Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

On 1 November 2001 the Court changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed Second Section (Rule 52 § 1).

By a decision of 3 September 2002 the Court declared the applications partly admissible, having in the meantime joined them on 28 November 2000.

The applicants and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants were born several years apart. The applicant in application number one (the First Applicant) is a medical specialist; the applicant in application number two (the Second Applicant) is a medical administrative professional. They resided in a particular town.

The applicants, together with the First Applicant’s spouse, were sued by an individual, a former patient of the First Applicant (referred to herein as the plaintiff), in respect of property in Florida. The writ was issued on 12 February 1986. Before the action came to trial, the plaintiff was adjudicated bankrupt on 9 August 1989 and the trustee in bankruptcy continued the action.

The trial was set down to commence in the High Court in August 1994 but was postponed until the end of September 1994 because the judge was not available for the length of time needed for the hearing.

On the fourth day of trial, while the plaintiff was being cross-examined, an adjournment was granted because the plaintiff claimed to feel unwell. In the event, it emerged that no medical justification could be given for the plaintiff's continued non-attendance, but nonetheless the plaintiff remained absent for the rest of the trial. Because of the delay caused by the plaintiff's non-attendance, the trial could not be concluded in September 1994 and had to be adjourned until January 1995. Judgment was given on 27 April 1995.

The judge accepted the argument of the applicants and the First Applicant's spouse that it was necessary to disregard the plaintiff's evidence because of the plaintiff's failure to remain for cross-examination. However, on the basis of the documentary evidence and that adduced by the applicants and the First Applicant's spouse, the judge found that the transfer of the Florida property should be set aside on the ground that the defendants had not been able to rebut the presumption of undue influence created by the discrepancy in the price they paid the plaintiff for the property and its value at the time of the transfer, together with the medical professional-patient relationship between the First Applicant and the plaintiff.

The formal record of the judge’s order was not prepared by the parties until 26 October 1995. The applicants and the First Applicant's spouse applied to the Court of Appeal for leave to appeal on 1 November 1995. The appeal bundles were lodged on 4 October 1996. On 15 January 1997 the appeal was listed for hearing, and it was heard on 16 and 17 April 1997, on which last date the Court of Appeal gave judgment dismissing the appeal.

In connection with the moneys paid by the First Applicant for the property, the Court of Appeal confirmed the decision of the trial judge that the sum should be repaid, but that no interest should be paid on it. The trial judge had based his decision on the fact that the First Applicant “had had possession of the properties and receipt of the rents since the completion of the sale, subject only to the agreement to allow the plaintiff to use number 212 in the early months”. A specific justice regarded this as a “rough and ready result”, but one which would “reduce the opportunities for further dispute between the parties”.

On 19 April 1997 the applicants and the First Applicant's spouse were refused leave by the Court of Appeal to appeal to the House of Lords. On 30 March 1998 the applicants and the First Applicant's spouse were refused leave to appeal by the House of Lords.
results.final_text:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in two applications (application numbers one and two) against the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by two nationals of the respondent State, the First Applicant (introduced on 4 May 1998) and the Second Applicant (introduced on 12 August 1998).

The United Kingdom Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, a representative of the Government of the Foreign and Commonwealth Office.

The applicants alleged that the proceedings in which they were sued in respect of property in Florida were not dealt with within a reasonable time.

The applications were transmitted to the Court on 1 November 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The applications were allocated to the Third Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

On 1 November 2001 the Court changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed Second Section (Rule 52 § 1).

By a decision of 3 September 2002 the Court declared the applications partly admissible, having in the meantime joined them on 28 November 2000.

The applicants and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants were born several years apart. The applicant in application number one (the First Applicant) is a medical specialist; the applicant in application number two (the Second Applicant) is a medical administrative professional. They resided in a particular town.

The applicants, together with the First Applicant’s spouse, were sued by an individual, a former patient of the First Applicant (referred to herein as the plaintiff), in respect of property in Florida. The writ was issued on 12 February 1986. Before the action came to trial, the plaintiff was adjudicated bankrupt on 9 August 1989 and the trustee in bankruptcy continued the action.

The trial was set down to commence in the High Court in August 1994 but was postponed until the end of September 1994 because the judge was not available for the length of time needed for the hearing.

On the fourth day of trial, while the plaintiff was being cross-examined, an adjournment was granted because the plaintiff claimed to feel unwell. In the event, it emerged that no medical justification could be given for the plaintiff's continued non-attendance, but nonetheless the plaintiff remained absent for the rest of the trial. Because of the delay caused by the plaintiff's non-attendance, the trial could not be concluded in September 1994 and had to be adjourned until January 1995. Judgment was given on 27 April 1995.

The judge accepted the argument of the applicants and the First Applicant's spouse that it was necessary to disregard the plaintiff's evidence because of the plaintiff's failure to remain for cross-examination. However, on the basis of the documentary evidence and that adduced by the applicants and the First Applicant's spouse, the judge found that the transfer of the Florida property should be set aside on the ground that the defendants had not been able to rebut the presumption of undue influence created by the discrepancy in the price they paid the plaintiff for the property and its value at the time of the transfer, together with the medical professional-patient relationship between the First Applicant and the plaintiff.

The formal record of the judge’s order was not prepared by the parties until 26 October 1995. The applicants and the First Applicant's spouse applied to the Court of Appeal for leave to appeal on 1 November 1995. The appeal bundles were lodged on 4 October 1996. On 15 January 1997 the appeal was listed for hearing, and it was heard on 16 and 17 April 1997, on which last date the Court of Appeal gave judgment dismissing the appeal.

In connection with the moneys paid by the First Applicant for the property, the Court of Appeal confirmed the decision of the trial judge that the sum should be repaid, but that no interest should be paid on it. The trial judge had based his decision on the fact that the First Applicant “had had possession of the properties and receipt of the rents since the completion of the sale, subject only to the agreement to allow the plaintiff to use number 212 in the early months”. A specific justice regarded this as a “rough and ready result”, but one which would “reduce the opportunities for further dispute between the parties”.

On 19 April 1997 the applicants and the First Applicant's spouse were refused leave by the Court of Appeal to appeal to the House of Lords. On 30 March 1998 the applicants and the First Applicant's spouse were refused leave to appeal by the House of Lords.
